patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 0/6] btrfs: delay compression to bbio submission time
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Proposal to delay compression in btrfs until bbio submission time, aiming to improve performance and reduce complexity.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Expressed concerns about the complexity of implementing the proposed solution, suggesting an iterative split approach as a possible alternative. Warned that if the implementation becomes too difficult, it's okay to abandon the effort.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the possibility of using an ordered workqueue to address some issues, but expressed concerns about the sequential writeback assumption being broken due to memory pressure. Indicated a willingness to try out some ideas mentioned in the discussion.
review_comments.raw_body:
在 2026/4/2 11:21, Boris Burkov 写道:
> On Thu, Apr 02, 2026 at 10:45:14AM +1030, Qu Wenruo wrote:
>>
>>
>> 在 2026/4/2 09:59, Qu Wenruo 写道:
>> [...]
>>>> So
>>>> we are breaking the contract "OE <=> allocated space" to allow this. I
>>>> think making the absolute core of the improvement more apparent in the
>>>> descriptions would be helpful.
>>>>
>>>> I think one thing I still don't understand is the desire for the layered
>>>> bios/OEs instead of creating the same delayed OE, but then as we do
>>>> the real
>>>> allocation/compression and discover the actual ranges doing
>>>> btrfs_split_ordered_extent() like short DIO writes, which seems quite
>>>> similar. Splitting/joining feels like a much more natural model for
>>>> ranges like OEs than layering into a tree. As we discover the sub ranges
>>>> we actually use, we split off the real OE.
>>>
>>> I can definitely work towards that direction. Although my concern is the
>>> OE waiting/start part and error handling.
>>>
>>> But so far those are only concerns, I need to implement the code to see
>>> what can go wrong.
>>>
>>> And if no major problem is hit, you can see a v2 with the split solution.
>>
>> Finally I recall the challenge using btrfs_split_ordered_extent(), that we
>> can not split the OE in the middle.
>>
>> E.g. we have a delayed OE for range [0, 32K), then due to whatever reasons
>> (e.g. memory pressure), we are forced to submit range [0, 16K) first, then
>> range [16K, 32K).
>>
>> Both go through delayed compression, but the range [16K, 32K) win the race
>> by failing the compression (bad ratio), and fallback to uncompressed
>> submission first, before range [0, 16K) even finishes its compression.
>>
>> Furthermore, for the range [16K, 32K) we do not have a large enough free
>> space to fill it in one go, but can only allocate several 8K sized extents.
>>
>> So we need to split the [16K, 32K) into two ranges, [16K, 24K) and [24K,
>> 32K).
>>
>> This means we have to split the original [0, 32K) extent into [0, 16K),
>> [16K, 24K) and [24K, 32K) ranges.
>> This is not supported by the current btrfs_split_ordered_extent(), which can
>> only split range from the beginning of an OE.
>>
>>
>> I'll try to implement a version of btrfs_split_ordered_extent() that can
>> split the range at any offset to see how things will work then.
> 
> This was kinda buggy before with the extent maps, and I think Naohiro
> Christoph and I ended up doing a good amount of refactoring to get rid
> of it. I am confident you can implement it correctly, just a fair warning.
> 
> Also, you could consider doing an iterative split? Like if you need 16k,
> then split [0,128k) into [0,16k)[16k,128k) then split [16k,128k) into
> [16k,24k)[24k,128k).

Yes, that's possible if we go the ordered workqueue, and as long as the 
writeback is always happening sequentially.

But the sequential writeback may not always be true.

E.g. we are initially writebacking folio 0, which created the OE for 
range [0, 128K), but by memory pressure or whatever in MM layer, 
suddenly the MM chose also to trigger writeback for folio 64K, meanwhile 
our initial writeback is still submitting folios for range [0, 32k).

In that case, we have to do the split for 64K, and the sequential nature 
is broken no matter what.

And that's also why I believe we hold all folios locked in the existing 
async submission path, to avoid such unexpected split caused by MM.

I will definitely try some aspects of the ideas mentioned here, like 
using ordered workqueue (which also addresses the current out-of-order 
OE disk bytenr problems).

But if the OE splits is too complex, I'll definitely give you some 
feedback about the problems.

Thanks,
Qu

> 
> Not sure if that is easier than the "true" 3 (or K) way split.
> 
> If all this becomes a huge headache please feel don't feel obliged to
> carry on forever, I am happy to discuss again.
> 
> Thanks,
> Boris
> 
>>
>> Thanks,
>> Qu
>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 1/2] firmware_loader: add search_path= module option for multi-path firmware lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new module option called firmware_class.search_path= that allows users to specify multiple paths where the kernel should search for firmware files. The input is preprocessed at boot or sysfs write time into a NUL-separated sequence of paths, avoiding per-load parsing overhead. The lookup order prioritizes the specified paths over built-in default paths and legacy single-path configurations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 2/2] selftests/firmware: add search path test for firmware_class.search_path=
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new selftest, fw_search.sh, to validate the firmware_class.search_path= module parameter. The test covers various scenarios, including firmware found in different search directories, priority of path= over search_path=, and handling of escaped characters in directory names. The test also checks that an empty search_path= does not break firmware loading and that sysfs readback matches what was written.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch allows the firmware_class.path to accept multiple paths, enabling more flexible firmware loading. The change introduces a new module option called search_path= that can be used in addition to or instead of path=. This is achieved by modifying the firmware_loader code and adding selftests to verify the functionality. The patch addresses previous review comments and includes several changes from earlier versions, including increasing the length of the search string and preprocessing the search path.

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] mm: fix IOCB_DONTCACHE write performance with rate-limited writeback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses performance issues caused by the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag, which triggers aggressive flushing of dirty pages on every write operation. The problem arises when multiple writers contend for writeback submission, leading to severe serialization and reduced throughput. To fix this, a new function filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() is introduced, which uses two rate-limiting mechanisms: skip-if-busy (checking if writeback is already in progress) and proportional cap (limiting the number of pages flushed). These mechanisms prevent excessive flushing and contention between concurrent writers. The patch replaces the existing filemap_flush_range() call with the new function in generic_write_sync().

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Christoph Hellwig questioned the connection between the patch's first and second paragraphs, expressing uncertainty about the relevance of the initial explanation to the proposed solution.

Reviewer Christoph Hellwig questioned the necessity of the patch, pointing out that filemap_flush_range and filemap_writeback already cap the number of written pages to the range passed in, making the proposed change unclear.

Reviewer noted that the new filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() function, introduced to fix IOCB_DONTCACHE write performance issues, has an incorrect number of arguments compared to the existing filemap_writeback() function.

I'm not sure the first how you think the first paragraph relate to
the second.

pages to the range passed in.  What do you think is the change here?

including linux-next.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Ritesh suspects that the performance penalty in a single sequential writer is due to contention between the submission side and completion side on the xa_lock, which is exacerbated by IOCB_DONTCACHE's behavior of skipping flushes and allowing more dirty pages to be written before completion. He suggests adding a perf callgraph to visualize this contention.

The reviewer noted that the new filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() function may not correctly handle cases where a subsequent writer does not write to the same range as the previous writer, potentially leaving dirty pages unflushed until background writeback occurs.

Reviewer Ritesh questioned whether the patch was properly rebased and pointed out an error in the filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() function call, specifically mentioning a potentially incorrect argument
review_comments.raw_body:
I am not sure if this 2.5x performance penalty in a "single" sequential
writer is due to throttling logic. On giving it some thoughts, I suspect
if this is because, the submission side and the completion side both
takes the xa_lock and hence could be contending on that.

For e.g. since this patch skips doing the flush the second time, (note
that writeback is active when the same writer dirtied the page during
previous write), this allows the writer to do more work of writing data
to page cache pages, instead of waiting on the xa_lock which the
completion callback could be holding (folio_end_writeback() -> folio_end_dropbehind())

If I see Peak Dirty data from the link you shared [1] in single writer case...

Mode                    MB/s	p50 (ms)	p99 (ms)	p99.9 (ms)	Peak Dirty	Peak Cache
dontcache (unpatched)	1179	3.2	    103.3	    170.9	    14 MB	    4.7 GB
dontcache (patched)	1453	5.4	    43.8	    57.4	    36 GB	    45 GB

... this too shows that the submission side is writing more dirty pages,
then the completion side able to write it... 

I suspect this contention (between submission and completion) could more
in IOCB_DONTCACHE case, since the completion side also removes the folio
from the page cache within the same xa_lock, which is not the same with
normal buffered writes.

Maybe a perf callgraph showing the contention would be nicer thing to add
here [1] ;). 

[1]: https://markdownpastebin.com/?id=96249deb897a401ba32acbce05312dcc

---

Yes, but the next writer may not write the dirty pages, of the previous
writer which skipped the flush call right (even if it finds the tag
clear)? Because filemap_dontcache_writeback_range( ) passes the range
and nr_to_write that means, unless the previous writer dirtied the same
range, the new writer won't be able to write the dirty pages of the
previous writer correct? So, it is mainly only the background writeback
now, which will flush this dirty pages of the writer which skipped the
flush (unless of course a fsync/sync call is made).

But having said that, I agree, this patch series is a nice performance
improvement overall :)

---

Was this rebased against some other tree? I couldn't find it in
linux-next. I think, that last argument is wrong.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged a reviewer's point about potential contention for xa_lock, agreed to investigate further using perf tools.

The author acknowledged a concern about the effectiveness of relying on VM dirty limits when DONTCACHE writers skip flushing and explained that they tried an alternative approach but it performed worse, indicating no immediate plan to revisit this issue.

Author acknowledged that their previous patch contained an error, promised to fix it in a revised version (v2) and will resubmit soon.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's an interesting point.

The theory I've been operating on is that the flusher thread ends up
squatting on the xa_lock for a while when memory gets tight, and that
blocks other readers and writers. Staying ahead of the dirty limits and
limiting the amount of flush work that each writer does alleviates
contention for that lock and that's what improves the performance.

You're right though. I'll plan to play around with perf and see if I
can confirm the theory.

---

Correct. When DONTCACHE writers end up skipping the flush, we rely on
VM dirty limits to eventually take care of flushing the data that got
skipped. That's why the DONTCACHE dirty pagecache max size ends up
looking close to buffered mode's.

I did play with a patch that had the writers attempt to flush 4x more
than they had written when memory was tight to compensate for that, but
it ended up performing worse than this set. It's possible that tuning
that down to 2x or so would do better, but I decided to just stop here
and post what I had.

---

Yes, my apologies. I think this must have been a bad merge on my part
during the rebase. I'll post a v2 in the near future.

The belief is that under heavy parallel write workload on the same
inode, the writers all end up stacking up on the mapping's xa_lock.
However as Ritesh points out, I should probably confirm that with perf.

---

I had some earlier results that indicated that this did help. It's
possible they were bogus though. I'll recheck that and get back to you.

---

Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: add atomic flush guard for IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds an atomic flush guard to prevent a 'thundering herd' problem that causes high tail latency when multiple IOCB_DONTCACHE writers submit flushes simultaneously after the PAGECACHE_TAG_WRITEBACK tag clears. The patch introduces an AS_DONTCACHE_FLUSHING flag and uses test_and_set_bit() to ensure only one writer flushes at a time, while also adding a dirty pressure escape hatch that forces extra flushes when dirty pages exceed 75% of the dirty_ratio threshold.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Christoph Hellwig expressed concern about a potential lock ordering issue and suggested alternative approaches to handling IOCB_DONTCACHE writes, including disabling in-caller writeback or queuing up wb_writeback_work instances.
review_comments.raw_body:
This sounds like a bad reimplementation of the single writeback thread
:)

Have you considered stopping to do in-caller writeback for
IOCB_DONTCACHE vs just leaving it to the writeback daeon?

Either by totally disabling the writeback and just leaving the
dropbehind bit, or by queuing up wb_writeback_work instances for
the ranges, or by just increasing the pressure for the writeback
daemon.  Note that with all schemes including the one in this patch
we might eventually run into writeback scalability limits, which
will require multiple writeback workers.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the current implementation of 'dropbehind' mode is not sufficient to address tail latency issues, and plans to explore a new approach where each writer submits writeback work without lock contention, potentially benefiting both IOCB_DONTCACHE and normal buffered writes.
review_comments.raw_body:
I did test a "dropbehind" mode that just set the dropbehind bit without
doing the flush at the end of the write. It was better than stock
dontcache but the tail latencies were still pretty bad.

I think having each writer do some writeback submission work makes a
lot of sense. It helps keep the dirty pages below the dirty thresholds
and doesn't seem to tax each writing task _too_ much. The trick is
avoiding lock contention while doing it.

I think what would be ideal would be to have some (lockless) mechanism
to say "there is enough data touched by the range just written to kick
off a write that's a suitable size for the backing store". Each writer
could check that and then kick off writeback for an approprite range.

I think this even could be beneficial in the normal buffered write
codepath too.

Anyway, I'll play around with this idea some more and come back with a
v2.

Thanks for the review!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] testing: add nfsd-io-bench NFS server benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a benchmark suite called nfsd-io-bench to test the performance of NFSD I/O modes using fio and the libnfs backend against an NFS server on localhost. The suite includes tests for buffered, don'tcache, and direct I/O modes via NFSD debugfs controls. It also includes scripts to orchestrate the test matrix, parse results from fio JSON output, and configure the NFS export for testing. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive benchmarking tool for evaluating NFSD I/O performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] testing: add dontcache-bench local filesystem benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite called 'dontcache-bench' to test the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag on local filesystems using fio's io_uring engine. The suite includes various job files and scripts to run and parse the results of these tests, mirroring the existing nfsd-io-bench test matrix but with different I/O modes. The goal is to evaluate the performance impact of disabling caching for I/O operations on local filesystems.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patchset improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by addressing flush contention issues that occur during heavy write activity. The patches modify the Linux kernel's mm module to alleviate this contention, resulting in better performance for writes. The changes were tested with various workloads and IO modes, including buffered, direct, and unpatched dontcache, and showed significant improvements over the original RWF_DONTCACHE implementation. The patchset also includes benchmarking scripts to demonstrate the results of these tests.

perspective i.e. comparing unpatched v/s patched RWF_DONTCACHE mode.

---

That's right for comparison between buffered write against RWF_DONTCACHE.
But what I meant in above was for the improvement from 1179 MB/s to 1453
MB/s could be accounted to less contention on xa_lock on patched version
v/s unpatched version for single write sequential testcase.

---

Yes, thanks, that will be nice to have!

-ritesh

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 00/13] nfsd/sunrpc: add support for netlink upcalls for mountd/exportd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for netlink upcalls to the NFS daemon (nfsd) and sunrpc cache mechanism, replacing antiquated interfaces in /proc. The new netlink-based interfaces allow for easier extension of export options and better container compatibility. A companion userland patch series is also included to implement these changes in mountd/exportd and exportfs.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)

review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the naming of sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout(), which was renamed to sunrpc_cache_upcall_warn() due to its purpose being unrelated to timeouts, but rather to warn if there are no listeners.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the timing of netlink notifications for pending cache requests, explaining that a later patch will add a new cache_notify operation to struct cache_detail and handle sending the notification when there is a pending cache_request.

review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about exposing the cache_request structure outside of cache.c by adding two new functions, sunrpc_cache_requests_count() and sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(), which allow callers to count and snapshot pending upcall requests without exposing the struct. The functions take care to skip entries that no longer have CACHE_PENDING set and provide a reference on each cache_head via cache_get().

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed the concern that auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid caches cannot use the nfsd generic netlink family because they live in the sunrpc module. The author created a new 'sunrpc' generic netlink family with its own multicast group to support cache upcall notifications for these caches.
sentiment_signals: author addressed a specific technical concern
review_comments.raw_body:
The auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid caches live in the sunrpc module,
so they cannot use the nfsd generic netlink family. Create a new
"sunrpc" generic netlink family with its own "exportd" multicast
group to support cache upcall notifications for sunrpc-resident
caches.

Define a YAML spec (sunrpc_cache.yaml) with a cache-type enum
(ip_map, unix_gid), a cache-notify multicast event, and the
corresponding uapi header.

Implement sunrpc_cache_notify() in cache.c, which checks for
listeners on the exportd multicast group, builds and sends a
SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY message with the cache-type attribute.

Register/unregister the sunrpc_nl_family in init_sunrpc() and
cleanup_sunrpc().

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml | 40 ++++++++++++++++++++++++
 include/linux/sunrpc/cache.h                  |  2 ++
 include/uapi/linux/sunrpc_netlink.h           | 35 +++++++++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/Makefile                           |  2 +-
 net/sunrpc/cache.c                            | 44 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/netlink.c                          | 34 +++++++++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/netlink.h                          | 22 ++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/sunrpc_syms.c                      | 10 ++++++
 8 files changed, 188 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml b/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..f4aa699598bca9ce0215bbc418d9ddcae25c0110
--- /dev/null
+++ b/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml
@@ -0,0 +1,40 @@
+# SPDX-License-Identifier: ((GPL-2.0 WITH Linux-syscall-note) OR BSD-3-Clause)
+---
+name: sunrpc
+protocol: genetlink
+uapi-header: linux/sunrpc_netlink.h
+
+doc: SUNRPC cache upcall support over generic netlink.
+
+definitions:
+  -
+    type: flags
+    name: cache-type
+    entries: [ip_map, unix_gid]
+
+attribute-sets:
+  -
+    name: cache-notify
+    attributes:
+      -
+        name: cache-type
+        type: u32
+        enum: cache-type
+
+operations:
+  list:
+    -
+      name: cache-notify
+      doc: Notification that there are cache requests that need servicing
+      attribute-set: cache-notify
+      mcgrp: exportd
+      event:
+        attributes:
+          - cache-type
+
+mcast-groups:
+  list:
+    -
+      name: none
+    -
+      name: exportd

index f88dc6bb17c7078781b3cf7e0371f369eddcb563..2735c332ddb736ef043f811b5e9e6ad2f57c9ce7 100644
--- a/include/linux/sunrpc/cache.h
+++ b/include/linux/sunrpc/cache.h
@@ -256,6 +256,8 @@ int sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(struct cache_detail *cd,
 				   struct cache_head **items,
 				   u64 *seqnos, int max,
 				   u64 min_seqno);
+int sunrpc_cache_notify(struct cache_detail *cd, struct cache_head *h,
+			u32 cache_type);
 
 /* Must store cache_detail in seq_file->private if using next three functions */
 extern void *cache_seq_start_rcu(struct seq_file *file, loff_t *pos);

new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..6135d9b3eef155a9192d9710c8c690283ec49073
--- /dev/null
+++ b/include/uapi/linux/sunrpc_netlink.h
@@ -0,0 +1,35 @@
+/* SPDX-License-Identifier: ((GPL-2.0 WITH Linux-syscall-note) OR BSD-3-Clause) */
+/* Do not edit directly, auto-generated from: */
+/*	Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml */
+/* YNL-GEN uapi header */
+/* To regenerate run: tools/net/ynl/ynl-regen.sh */
+
+#ifndef _UAPI_LINUX_SUNRPC_NETLINK_H
+#define _UAPI_LINUX_SUNRPC_NETLINK_H
+
+#define SUNRPC_FAMILY_NAME	"sunrpc"
+#define SUNRPC_FAMILY_VERSION	1
+
+enum sunrpc_cache_type {
+	SUNRPC_CACHE_TYPE_IP_MAP = 1,
+	SUNRPC_CACHE_TYPE_UNIX_GID = 2,
+};
+
+enum {
+	SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_CACHE_TYPE = 1,
+
+	__SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_MAX,
+	SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_MAX = (__SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_MAX - 1)
+};
+
+enum {
+	SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY = 1,
+
+	__SUNRPC_CMD_MAX,
+	SUNRPC_CMD_MAX = (__SUNRPC_CMD_MAX - 1)
+};
+
+#define SUNRPC_MCGRP_NONE	"none"
+#define SUNRPC_MCGRP_EXPORTD	"exportd"
+
+#endif /* _UAPI_LINUX_SUNRPC_NETLINK_H */
diff --git a/net/sunrpc/Makefile b/net/sunrpc/Makefile
index f89c10fe7e6acc71d47273200d85425a2891a08a..96727df3aa85435a2de63a8483eab9d75d5b3495 100644
--- a/net/sunrpc/Makefile
+++ b/net/sunrpc/Makefile
@@ -14,7 +14,7 @@ sunrpc-y := clnt.o xprt.o socklib.o xprtsock.o sched.o \
 	    addr.o rpcb_clnt.o timer.o xdr.o \
 	    sunrpc_syms.o cache.o rpc_pipe.o sysfs.o \
 	    svc_xprt.o \
-	    xprtmultipath.o
+	    xprtmultipath.o netlink.o
 sunrpc-$(CONFIG_SUNRPC_DEBUG) += debugfs.o
 sunrpc-$(CONFIG_SUNRPC_BACKCHANNEL) += backchannel_rqst.o
 sunrpc-$(CONFIG_PROC_FS) += stats.o
diff --git a/net/sunrpc/cache.c b/net/sunrpc/cache.c
index 1282c61030d35efd924072e2739109cfae3472e2..d477b19dbfa15fdff71f946ade2643b56c35d491 100644
--- a/net/sunrpc/cache.c
+++ b/net/sunrpc/cache.c
@@ -33,9 +33,11 @@
 #include <linux/sunrpc/cache.h>
 #include <linux/sunrpc/stats.h>
 #include <linux/sunrpc/rpc_pipe_fs.h>
+#include <net/genetlink.h>
 #include <trace/events/sunrpc.h>
 
 #include "netns.h"
+#include "netlink.h"
 #include "fail.h"
 
 #define	 RPCDBG_FACILITY RPCDBG_CACHE
@@ -1960,3 +1962,45 @@ int sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(struct cache_detail *cd,
 	return i;
 }
 EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot);
+
+/**
+ * sunrpc_cache_notify - send a netlink notification for a cache event
+ * @cd: cache_detail for the cache
+ * @h: cache_head entry (unused, reserved for future use)
+ * @cache_type: cache type identifier (e.g. SUNRPC_CACHE_TYPE_UNIX_GID)
+ *
+ * Sends a SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY multicast message on the "exportd"
+ * group if any listeners are present. Returns 0 on success or a
+ * negative errno.
+ */
+int sunrpc_cache_notify(struct cache_detail *cd, struct cache_head *h,
+			u32 cache_type)
+{
+	struct genlmsghdr *hdr;
+	struct sk_buff *msg;
+
+	if (!genl_has_listeners(&sunrpc_nl_family, cd->net,
+				SUNRPC_NLGRP_EXPORTD))
+		return -ENOLINK;
+
+	msg = genlmsg_new(nla_total_size(sizeof(u32)), GFP_KERNEL);
+	if (!msg)
+		return -ENOMEM;
+
+	hdr = genlmsg_put(msg, 0, 0, &sunrpc_nl_family, 0,
+			  SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY);
+	if (!hdr) {
+		nlmsg_free(msg);
+		return -ENOMEM;
+	}
+
+	if (nla_put_u32(msg, SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_CACHE_TYPE, cache_type)) {
+		nlmsg_free(msg);
+		return -ENOMEM;
+	}
+
+	genlmsg_end(msg, hdr);
+	return genlmsg_multicast_netns(&sunrpc_nl_family, cd->net, msg, 0,
+				       SUNRPC_NLGRP_EXPORTD, GFP_KERNEL);
+}
+EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(sunrpc_cache_notify);

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/13] nfsd: move struct nfsd_genl_rqstp to nfsctl.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the definition of the struct nfsd_genl_rqstp from the header file nfsd.h to the source file nfsctl.c, where it is used. This simplifies the code and reduces the risk of inconsistencies between the header and source files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/13] sunrpc: rename sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall() in preparation for its future use of netlink for upcalls, if configured. The rename is applied across four files: fs/nfsd/export.c, include/linux/sunrpc/cache.h, net/sunrpc/cache.c, and net/sunrpc/svcauth_unix.c. The patch makes 7 insertions and 7 deletions to update function calls and declarations accordingly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 03/13] sunrpc: rename sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout() to sunrpc_cache_upcall_warn(). The original function name was misleading as it did not actually handle timeouts, but instead warned if there were no listeners. This change clarifies the purpose of the function and makes its name more descriptive. The rename affects 6 files in total, with 9 insertions and 9 deletions across these files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 04/13] sunrpc: rename cache_pipe_upcall() to cache_do_upcall()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function cache_pipe_upcall() to cache_do_upcall() in anticipation of a future netlink-based upcall mechanism. The renaming is done to make the code more flexible and prepare it for upcoming changes. This change does not introduce any new functionality but rather sets the stage for future improvements.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/13] sunrpc: add a cache_notify callback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new callback function, cache_notify, to the struct cache_detail in the sunrpc module. The purpose of this callback is to notify when there is a pending cache_request, which will be used by a later patch that sends netlink notifications for such requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 06/13] sunrpc: add helpers to count and snapshot pending cache requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new functions, sunrpc_cache_requests_count() and sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(), to the Linux kernel's SunRPC implementation. These functions allow callers to count and snapshot the pending upcall request list without exposing internal data structures. The count function returns the number of requests with CACHE_PENDING set, while the snapshot function takes a reference on each item so it can be safely used after the queue lock is released. This patch will be used by the nfsd generic netlink dumpit handler for svc_export upcall requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 07/13] sunrpc: add a generic netlink family for cache upcalls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a generic netlink family called 'sunrpc' to support cache upcall notifications for sunrpc-resident caches. It defines a YAML specification, implements the necessary functions in the sunrpc module, and registers/unregisters the new netlink family during initialization and cleanup. The goal is to enable cache upcalls for the auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid caches that live within the sunrpc module.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 08/13] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.ip cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the ip_map (auth.unix.ip) cache in the sunrpc subsystem. It introduces new attributes and operations to the sunrpc_cache YAML spec, including ip-map attribute-set and ip-map-reqs wrapper. The patch also implements two new functions: sunrpc_nl_ip_map_get_reqs_dumpit() to snapshot pending requests and send them over netlink, and sunrpc_nl_ip_map_set_reqs_doit() to parse responses from userspace and update the cache. Additionally, it wires up the ip_map_notify() callback in ip_map_cache_template to trigger SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY multicast events on cache misses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 09/13] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.gid cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for netlink-based cache upcalls for the unix_gid (auth.unix.gid) cache in the sunrpc subsystem. It introduces new attributes and operations to the sunrpc_cache YAML spec, including a new attribute-set for unix_gids and two new operations: unix-gid-get-reqs and unix-gid-set-reqs. The patch also implements netlink-based upcalls for these operations, allowing userspace to dump pending requests and respond to them. This is done by introducing new functions that snapshot pending requests, parse responses from userspace, and update the cache accordingly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 10/13] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the svc_export cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support to the NFS daemon (nfsd) for its svc_export cache. It introduces a new netlink event, NFSD_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY, which is sent when there are pending requests in the cache. The event carries information about the cache type and allows userspace to handle the notifications. The patch also includes changes to the nfsd code to wire up the notification mechanism and update the cache based on user responses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 11/13] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the nfsd.fh cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the nfsd.fh cache, allowing clients to notify the server of changes to cached file handles. The patch introduces a new 'expkey' attribute-set and operations to the nfsd YAML spec, implements netlink message handling functions, and wires up the expkey_notify callback to trigger multicast events when cache misses occur.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 12/13] sunrpc: add SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH generic netlink command that allows userspace to flush the sunrpc auth caches (ip_map and unix_gid) without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command takes an optional attribute, SUNRPC_A_CACHE_FLUSH_MASK, which specifies which caches to flush. If omitted, all sunrpc caches are flushed. This is used by exportfs to replace its /proc-based cache_flush() with a netlink equivalent, with /proc fallback for older kernels.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new netlink command, NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH, which allows userspace to flush the nfsd export caches without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command takes an optional attribute, NFSD_A_CACHE_FLUSH_MASK, that specifies which caches to flush. If omitted, all nfsd caches are flushed. This is used by exportfs to replace its /proc-based cache_flush() with a netlink equivalent, and provides a fallback to /proc for older kernels.

Re: [PATCH 1/4] mm: fix IOCB_DONTCACHE write performance with rate-limited writeback
Re: [PATCH 1/4] mm: fix IOCB_DONTCACHE write performance with rate-limited writeback
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch aims to improve write performance when using IOCB_DONTCACHE, a flag that prevents caching of writes. The issue arises from the flusher thread contending for the xa_lock when memory is tight, which can block readers and writers. To alleviate this contention, the patch limits the amount of flush work done by each writer and stays ahead of dirty limits, resulting in improved performance.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 14/14] docs: fuse: add io-uring bufring and zero-copy documentation

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 14/14] docs: fuse: add io-uring bufring and zero-copy documentation

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 00/14] fuse: add io-uring buffer rings and zero-copy
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer ring and zero-copy capabilities to the FUSE file system over io-uring, reducing memory usage and enabling pinned buffers for future optimizations.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary: Moved fuse_uring_abort() out of the inline header definition and into dev_uring.c to make subsequent commits cleaner.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary: Rearranged io-uring iovec and ent allocation logic to make the subsequent commit that adds buffer rings easier to review.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/14] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching and sending the next request in the fuse kernel module, making the control flow clearer and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. The duplicated logic from fuse_uring_send() is removed, and a new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() is introduced to fetch the next request. The send operation is now handled by a separate function fuse_uring_send(). This change simplifies the code and improves maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/14] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers to a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts the header copying into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(), which makes the code more modular and easier to understand. The function handles errors and rate-limited logging, making it more robust. This change improves the maintainability of the code and reduces duplication.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 03/14] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module to make it more clear and centralized. It introduces a new function, copy_header_from_ring(), which handles the actual copying of data from user space to kernel space. This function includes error handling and rate-limited logging. The existing code is modified to use this new function instead of directly calling copy_from_user(). This change improves the maintainability and readability of the code by separating the header copying logic into a single, self-contained function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 04/14] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This change will simplify both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying when buffer rings are added, making it easier to manage and maintain the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/14] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up the copy state for payload copying in the FUSE file system. It introduces a new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state()` that encapsulates the logic for importing user buffers and initializing the copy state. This simplifies the code and makes it easier to maintain by reducing duplication and improving modularity.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 06/14] fuse: support buffer copying for kernel addresses
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for direct buffer copying in fuse, allowing it to work with kernel addresses instead of requiring page mapping and unmapping. This is a preparatory step for supporting pinned buffers in fuse-over-io-uring. The patch modifies the fuse_copy_init, fuse_copy_finish, fuse_copy_fill, and fuse_copy_do functions to handle both user-space and kernel-space buffer copying. It introduces a new 'is_kaddr' flag in the fuse_copy_state structure to indicate whether the buffer is a kernel address or not.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 07/14] fuse: use named constants for io-uring iovec indices
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces magic indices in the io-uring iovec array with named constants, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 08/14] fuse: move fuse_uring_abort() from header to dev_uring.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the `fuse_uring_abort()` function from a header file to a source file in the FUSE implementation, where it belongs due to its internal dependencies on other functions defined in that file. This change makes the code more organized and prepares for future additions of pinning capabilities for FUSE buffers. The function is responsible for aborting IO operations when necessary, such as during daemon termination.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 09/14] fuse: rearrange io-uring iovec and ent allocation logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch rearranges the allocation logic in the fuse_uring code to make it easier to review subsequent commits related to buffer rings. The function fuse_uring_get_iovec_from_sqe is moved to an earlier location in the file, and its allocation logic is moved to the beginning of the fuse_uring_create_ring_ent function. This change does not affect the existing logic but simplifies the code for future modifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 10/14] fuse: add io-uring buffer rings
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer rings to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring interface, allowing servers to communicate more efficiently by reducing memory usage and laying the groundwork for future optimizations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 11/14] fuse: add pinned headers capability for io-uring buffer rings
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the capability to pin header buffers for io-uring buffer rings in fuse servers. It introduces a new flag, FUSE_URING_PINNED_HEADERS, which can be set alongside FUSE_URING_BUFRING on REGISTER sqes. When enabled, the kernel pins the header pages and uses direct memcpy for copying, reducing per-request overhead. The patch also includes changes to account pinned pages against RLIMIT_MEMLOCK and track mm->pinned_vm. Unpinning is done in process context during connection abort.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 12/14] fuse: add pinned payload buffers capability for io-uring buffer rings
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the capability to pin payload buffers in io-uring buffer rings, allowing for direct memcpy of large payload copies and reducing overhead. The pinned pages are accounted against RLIMIT_MEMLOCK and unpinned during connection abort. This feature is particularly beneficial for high-performance systems and can result in a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads and other operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 13/14] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for zero-copy data transfer over io-uring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module, eliminating unnecessary memory copies between userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server for page-backed read/write operations. The feature requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and buffer rings with pinned headers and buffers. When enabled, the kernel registers client pages as a sparse buffer at the entry's fixed id via io_buffer_register_bvec(), allowing the fuse server to perform direct io-uring read/write operations on these pages. Non-page-backed arguments still go through the payload buffer as normal. The patch also includes details on the request flow for zero-copy write operations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module, specifically covering its usage of io-uring buffer rings and zero-copy techniques. The new documentation explains how FUSE leverages buffer rings to reduce memory usage and enable pinned buffers, as well as the high-level flow of request processing using these features. This patch aims to improve understanding and utilization of FUSE with io-uring by providing clear and concise documentation on its underlying mechanisms.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/4] io_uring: extend bvec registration

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/4] io_uring/rsrc: rename io_buffer_register_bvec()/io_buffer_unregister_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames two io_uring functions to better reflect their purpose and prepare for future changes. The function `io_buffer_register_bvec()` is renamed to `io_buffer_register_request()`, which will be used for registering buffers through a struct request. A subsequent patch will reuse the original name for direct bvec registration. Additionally, `io_buffer_unregister_bvec()` is renamed to `io_buffer_unregister()` to make it more generic and applicable to both buffer types. These changes are part of an effort to support kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/4] io_uring/rsrc: split io_buffer_register_request() logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits the main initialization logic in io_buffer_register_request() into a helper function called io_kernel_buffer_init(). The new function initializes kernel-populated buffers, which will be reused by fuse io-uring. This is a preparatory patch for supporting kernel-populated buffers and simplifies the code by separating the initialization logic from the main function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/4] io_uring/rsrc: add io_buffer_register_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function, io_buffer_register_bvec(), which allows registering an array of bio vectors (bvecs) to be used in the io_uring context. This is a preparatory step for implementing zero-copy support over fuse using io_uring. The function takes several parameters, including the io_uring command, the bvec array, and flags for issue and release. It initializes an io_mapped_ubuf structure and copies the provided bvecs into it. If successful, it returns 0; otherwise, it returns a negative error code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/4] io_uring/rsrc: rename and export IO_IMU_DEST / IO_IMU_SOURCE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames and exports two io_uring flags, IO_IMU_DEST and IO_IMU_SOURCE, to more descriptive names IO_BUF_DEST and IO_BUF_SOURCE. This change is necessary for the io_buffer_register_bvec() path, which requires the buffer to be both readable and writable. The patch updates several files, including include/linux/io_uring_types.h, io_uring/io_uring.c, io_uring/rsrc.c, and io_uring/rsrc.h, to reflect the new flag names and ensure they are exported for use by other subsystems.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series refactors and extends the io_uring registered buffers infrastructure to allow external subsystems to register pre-existing bvec arrays directly, which is a prerequisite for making fuse zero-copy possible.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v1] fuse: fix io-uring background queue stall on request completion

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1] fuse: fix io-uring background queue stall on request completion

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a stall in the io-uring background queue when a request completes. The issue arises from the fact that the connection-level background counters are not properly accounted for before flushing the queue's background queue, leaving pending requests stuck. To fix this, the patch accounts for the connection-level counters first and then flushes the queue's background queue, ensuring that pending requests are dispatched correctly.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer, Bernd Schubert, disagrees that the patch fixes a real issue and suggests an alternative solution to prevent background requests from getting stuck in the io-uring per-queue bg queue. He points out that fuse_uring_flush_bg() already allows at least one background request per queue to pass through when fc->active_background is below the limit, and proposes to always let through at least one request per queue.

The reviewer noted that the patch fixes an issue where only one request can run at a time, but did not provide further technical details or suggestions for improvement.

The reviewer noted that there might be a stuck background queue issue at teardown, and mentioned that Horst is investigating this separately.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think it ever gets stuck. In fuse_uring_flush_bg()

        while ((fc->active_background < fc->max_background ||
                !queue->active_background) &&


And queue->active_background gets decreased in the caller,
fuse_uring_req_end. Idea is to always let through at least one 
background request per queue. Reson is that the global 
fc->num_background might be at the limit already, a 
queue then might get a request, it would get added to 
queue->fuse_req_bg_queue, but once fc->active_background goes 
down, there wouldn't be anything to wake up these requests. 
Issue: Only one request allowed per queue when this comes up.

---

This basically solves the issue that situations could come up where only
one requests runs at a time.

---

Reviewed-by: Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com>



PS: If you should be chasing a stuck bg queue issue at tear down, Horst
is chasing a teardown issue with bg queue. 
We are currently testing this patch

diff --git a/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c b/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c
index 3de97ed2280f..451bf8981e19 100644
--- a/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c
+++ b/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c
@@ -136,11 +136,10 @@ void fuse_uring_abort_end_requests(struct fuse_ring *ring)
                if (!queue)
                        continue;

-               queue->stopped = true;
-
                WARN_ON_ONCE(ring->fc->max_background != UINT_MAX);
                spin_lock(&queue->lock);
                spin_lock(&fc->bg_lock);
+               queue->stopped = true;
                fuse_uring_flush_bg(queue);
                spin_unlock(&fc->bg_lock);
                spin_unlock(&queue->lock);


Thanks,
Bernd

Author acknowledges that the issue is being investigated by another developer and expresses appreciation for their work.
